l-hello.title: The magic question ending: -aano?
l-hello.intro: Malayalam turns a statement into a yes/no question just by adding -aano? to the end. No word order changes — much simpler than English!

points.heading: Statement → Question
points.body: ‘sukhamaanu’ = (you) are well. Add the question ending and it becomes ‘sukhamaano?’ = are you well?

points.heading: Hindi shortcut
points.body: Many words share roots with Hindi: ‘sukham’ = well-being (Hindi ‘sukh’), ‘nandi’ = thanks. Lean on what you already know.

l-yesno.title: Saying 'I want': enikku … venam
l-yesno.intro: To say you want something, Malayalam puts YOU in the dative ('to me') and ends with 'venam' (is wanted) — exactly like Hindi 'mujhe … chahiye'.

points.heading: The pattern
points.body: enikku (to me) + [thing] + venam (want). Drop 'venam' for 'venda' to say you DON'T want it.

points.heading: Two ways to say 'no'
points.body: ‘alla’ = it is NOT (that). ‘illa’ = there isn't / doesn't exist. Use ‘illa’ for absence: ‘vellam illa’ = there's no water.

l-clarify.title: Adding -illa to mean 'not'
l-clarify.intro: Stick -illa onto a verb to negate it. This single trick lets you flip any phrase you know into its negative.

points.heading: Flip it negative
points.body: ‘manassilaayi’ = (I) understood → ‘manassilaayilla’ = (I) didn't understand. ‘ariyaam’ = (I) know → ‘ariyilla’ = (I) don't know.

l-intro.title: 'My' and 'is': ente … aanu
l-intro.intro: Malayalam puts the verb at the END (Subject-Object-Verb). 'aanu' means 'is/am/are' and closes the sentence.

points.heading: Possessives
points.body: ‘ente’ = my, ‘ningalude’ = your. The owner comes first: ‘ente per’ = my name.

points.heading: Verb goes last
points.body: English: 'My name IS Ravi.' Malayalam: 'My name Ravi IS' — the 'aanu' (is) lands at the end. This SOV order is the #1 thing to get used to.

l-about-you.title: He, she, we
l-about-you.intro: Malayalam pronouns are simple, but politeness matters. Use 'ningal' (polite you) with anyone you don't know well.

points.heading: The basics
points.body: njaan = I, ningal = you (polite), avan = he, aval = she, nammal = we (including you).

l-restaurant.title: Polite requests: -aamo? and joining with -um
l-restaurant.intro: End a request with -aamo? to sound polite ('could you…?'), and link nouns with -um … -um ('both … and').

points.heading: Could you…?
points.body: ‘tharuka’ = give → ‘tharaamo?’ = could you give? Soft and polite, perfect for ordering.

points.heading: 'And' = -um … -um
points.body: Attach -um to each item: ‘choru-m saambaar-um’ = rice AND sambar.

l-directions.title: Where is …? and giving directions
l-directions.intro: Ask the location of anything with '… evideyaanu?' — just put the place at the front.

points.heading: The all-purpose question
points.body: [place] + evideyaanu? = where is [place]? Reuse it everywhere.

points.heading: Commands end in -uka
points.body: ‘pokuka’ = go, ‘thiriyuka’ = turn. ‘nere pokuka’ = go straight.

l-numbers.title: Counting in Malayalam
l-numbers.intro: The first ten numbers unlock prices, time, and quantities. A few sound close to other Indian languages.

points.heading: 1 to 10
points.body: onnu, randu, moonnu, naalu, anchu, aaru, ezhu, ettu, onpathu, pathu. Say them out loud — rhythm helps them stick.

l-bignum.title: Bigger numbers & bargaining
l-bignum.intro: ‘pathu’ (10), ‘irupathu’ (20), ‘nooru’ (100). Combine with the price question to shop confidently.

points.heading: How much?
points.body: ‘ithu ethra?’ = how much is this? If it's steep: ‘valare kooduthalaanu’ (too much) then ‘kuravu cheyyaamo?’ (reduce it?).

l-feelings.title: Feelings: enikku … aanu / undu
l-feelings.intro: Feelings also use the dative 'enikku' (to me). Pair it with 'aanu' (is) or 'undu' (there is/have).

points.heading: The pattern
points.body: enikku + [feeling] + aanu/undu. ‘enikku santhosham aanu’ = I'm happy. ‘enikku pedi undu’ = I'm scared.

l-health.title: Describing symptoms
l-health.intro: Same 'enikku … undu' pattern handles aches and illness — very useful at a pharmacy.

points.heading: I have a …
points.body: enikku + [symptom] + undu. ‘enikku pani undu’ = I have a fever. ‘enikku thalaveedana undu’ = I have a headache.

l-time.title: Talking about time
l-time.intro: A handful of time words let you make plans: innu (today), naale (tomorrow), innale (yesterday), ippol (now).

points.heading: When?
points.body: Drop these in front of any sentence. ‘naale pokaam’ = let's go tomorrow.

l-plans.title: Let's…: namukku … pokaam
l-plans.intro: Suggest doing something together with 'namukku' (let's) + verb ending in -aam.

points.heading: Make a suggestion
points.body: ‘namukku pokaam’ = let's go. Add a place: ‘namukku beach-il pokaam’ = let's go to the beach.

l-office-basics.title: Malayalam loves English words
l-office-basics.intro: At work, Malayalis mix in English constantly — 'meeting', 'mail', 'report', 'leave'. You already know half the vocabulary; just add a Malayalam tail.

points.heading: English word + Malayalam glue
points.body: Take the English noun and attach a Malayalam ending: 'meeting undu' (there's a meeting), 'mail ayachu' (sent the mail).

points.heading: 'kazhinjo?' = done?
points.body: Stick 'kazhinjo?' after anything to ask if it's finished: 'work kazhinjo?' (work done?), 'kazhichu kazhinjo?' (done eating?).

l-house-help.title: Gentle commands: end in -u / -oo
l-house-help.intro: To ask someone to do something kindly, end the verb in a soft '-u' or '-oo'. It's a polite request, not a bark.

points.heading: Please do this
points.body: vrithiyaakku (clean), kazhuku (wash), alakku (wash clothes), varoo (come). The '-u/-oo' softens it.

points.heading: 'mathi' = enough
points.body: 'mathi' / 'mathiyaayi' means 'enough / that's plenty' — the polite way to say stop.

l-repairs.title: Saying 'no X' with -illa
l-repairs.intro: To say something is missing or not working, reach for 'illa' — the all-purpose 'not / none' word.

points.heading: No water, no power
points.body: 'vellam illa' (no water), 'current illa' (no power), 'pani cheyyunnilla' (not working). 'illa' shuts the sentence down.

points.heading: 'kedaayi poyi' = broke down
points.body: For things that broke: 'AC kedaayi poyi', 'phone kedaayi poyi'. Then ask 'sheri aakkaamo?' (can you fix it?).

l-transit-board.title: Asking 'does it…?' with -umo?
l-transit-board.intro: Take a future verb ('pokum' = will go) and add '-o' to turn it into a yes/no question: 'pokumo?' = does it go?

points.heading: Will it…? / Does it…?
points.body: 'ee bus … pokumo?' = does this bus go…? 'varumo?' = will it come? Just add '-o' to the '-um' future.

examples.english: Does this bus go to Ernakulam?

points.heading: 'ethu' = which
points.body: 'ethu bus?' (which bus?), 'ethu platform?' (which platform?). Point and ask.

l-think.title: I think / I don't think
l-think.intro: Two everyday verbs carry your opinions: 'vicharikkuka' (to think) and 'thonnuka' (to feel / reckon).

points.heading: Stating a view
points.body: Lead with 'njaan vicharikkunnu' (I think) or 'ente abhipraayathil' (in my opinion), then make your point.

examples.english: In my opinion this is good.

points.heading: Disagreeing softly
points.body: 'enikku thonnunnilla' (I don't think so), 'sheri alla' (not right). Gentle, never blunt.

l-compare.title: 'kooduthal' = more / better
l-compare.intro: Malayalam makes comparatives the easy way: just put 'kooduthal' (more) in front. No special 'better' word to memorise.

points.heading: More = better
points.body: 'nallathu' (good) → 'kooduthal nallathu' (better). 'kooduthal ishtam' = like more.

points.heading: Handy verdicts
points.body: 'kuzhappam illa' (not bad / fine), 'mosham aanu' (it's bad), 'onnu pole' (the same).

l-movies.title: Past vs now: kandu / kelkkunnu
l-movies.intro: Talking about entertainment is great tense practice: what you saw (past) versus what you're hearing right now.

points.heading: Saw it (past -u)
points.body: 'njaan cinema kandu' (I saw a movie), 'padam nannaayirunnu' (the movie was good). The '-u'/'-irunnu' marks the past.

points.heading: Watching now (-unnu)
points.body: 'njaan paattu kelkkunnu' (I'm listening to songs). Suggest a plan: 'namukku padam kaanaam' (let's watch a movie).

l-tenses-past.title: The 'Innale' pattern — past = -i / -u
l-tenses-past.intro: Talking about the past? Anchor the sentence with 'innale' (yesterday) and end the action in an '-i' or '-u' sound.

points.heading: Yesterday = -i / -u
points.body: Most past verbs end in '-i' or '-u': poyi (went), kandu (saw), kazhichu (ate), vannu (came), paranju (said). Pair them with 'innale'.

points.heading: Hindi parallel
points.body: Like Hindi's 'gaya / khaaya', Malayalam marks the past by changing the verb's tail — no extra helper word needed.

l-tenses-future.title: The 'Naale' pattern — future = -um
l-tenses-future.intro: Want to talk about the future? Just add an '-um' sound to the action and lead with 'naale' (tomorrow).

points.heading: Tomorrow = -um
points.body: pokum (will go), kaanum (will see), kazhikkum (will eat), varum (will come), cheyyum (will do). One ending covers 'I / he / we will'.

points.heading: Same verb, three tenses
points.body: Watch one verb shift: cheythu (did) → cheyyum (will do) → cheyyunnu (doing). Learn the endings, not tables.

l-tenses-present.title: The 'Ippol' pattern — now = -unnu
l-tenses-present.intro: For what's happening right now, end the action in '-unnu' and lead with 'ippol' (now).

points.heading: Now = -unnu
points.body: pokunnu (going), kaanunnu (watching), kazhikkunnu (eating), varunnu (coming), cheyyunnu (doing). '-unnu' is the '-ing' of Malayalam.

points.heading: The big three
points.body: innale + -i/-u (past), ippol + -unnu (now), naale + -um (future). Three time words and three endings unlock every tense.
